22: A type: 'And the people of Israel went into the midst of the sea on dry ground, the waters being a wall to them on their right hand and on their left.' Paul explicitly calls the Red Sea crossing a baptismal type: 'all were baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea' (1 Corinthians 10:1-2). The structure is identical to Christian baptism: God's redemptive act through a threshold of water, from slavery into freedom, into covenant relationship. But the antitype is greater: baptism into Christ is death and resurrection with Christ (Romans 6:3-4), not merely deliverance from one earthly power. Moses led Israel through the sea; Christ leads his people through death itself.
1: YHWH tells Moses to turn back and camp by the sea — the divine setup for the decisive confrontation. A revelation of God: the apparent strategic mistake (camping in a vulnerable position with the sea at their back) is the deliberate divine arrangement that displays YHWH's power. The cross appears as defeat — the executed criminal, the abandoned leader — but is the deliberate divine arrangement that accomplishes what no victory could. The apparent vulnerability of the people of God is often the precondition for divine demonstration.
2: Camp by the sea before Pi-hahiroth — the people are hemmed in: sea before them, Pharaoh's army behind, mountains on both sides. The trap is set by YHWH himself, not Pharaoh. Theme: God positions his people in situations of humanly inescapable danger so that the rescue can only be attributed to him. This is the structure of the Incarnation itself: the Son takes on the full weight of sin, death, and condemnation — the full trap of human existence — so that the resurrection can only be attributed to divine power (Romans 1:4: 'declared to be the Son of God with power by his resurrection from the dead').
3: Pharaoh will say the Israelites are wandering in confusion — the enemy misreads the trap as his opportunity. 1 Corinthians 2:8: 'none of the rulers of this age understood this wisdom, for if they had, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory.' Pharaoh pursues what he thinks is a trapped, leaderless people; the rulers of this age crucify what they think is a failed messianic pretender. In both cases, the apparent defeat is the precise mechanism of the definitive victory.
4: YHWH will harden Pharaoh's heart and gain glory over him through Pharaoh — the hardening is in service of the divine purpose, not a frustration of it. A revelation of God: the stubbornness of the powers that oppose redemption is drawn into the fulfillment of the redemptive purpose. Romans 9:17 quotes Ex Exodus 9:16 ('I raised you up for this very purpose') to ground the argument that even the resistance of the unbelieving is within God's sovereign purpose. The cross was not the triumph of the Pharaoh-powers but their final undoing (Colossians 2:15).
5: Pharaoh changes his mind — the oppressor regrets releasing his workforce. The powers of this world always regret the liberty they allow the covenant people. Theme: the church's liberation from every form of bondage is regularly contested by the powers from which it was delivered. The resurrection of Jesus is the moment when the powers of this age — having executed their verdict — discover that the verdict was overturned. Pharaoh's change of mind is the type of every re-imposition of slavery that the resurrection definitively overcomes.
6: Pharaoh prepares his chariot and armies — the full military force of the greatest empire of the ancient world pursues a community of escaped slaves. A revelation of God by contrast: the display of military power is precisely what YHWH will overturn. The cross confronts the full military and judicial power of Rome; the resurrection overturns it entirely. 1 Corinthians 1:27-28: 'God chose the weak things of the world to shame the strong.' The chariot-force that follows Israel into the sea will be swallowed; the death-force that follows Jesus into the grave will be overwhelmed by the resurrection.
7: Six hundred chosen chariots plus all the other chariots of Egypt — the maximum military force. The power asymmetry between the Egyptian army and the liberated slaves is absolute; no human resistance is possible. Theme: the gospel operates precisely in this impossible situation. The power that operates in redemption is not human strength but divine intervention: 'Not by might nor by power, but by my Spirit' (Zechariah 4:6). The cross and resurrection are God's response to the full force of sin, death, and the law arrayed against humanity — not human resistance but divine reversal.
8: YHWH hardened Pharaoh's heart so he pursued the Israelites, who were going out boldly (beyad ramah) — with a high hand, openly. The covenant community's bold, public departure is the form the exodus takes. Theme: the new covenant community does not escape from death and sin secretly but is raised openly with Christ — 'you died, and your life is now hidden with Christ in God. When Christ, who is your life, appears, then you also will appear with him in glory' (Colossians 3:3-4). The high-hand departure of Israel anticipates the public vindication of the resurrection.
9: The Egyptians overtook them by the sea — the apparently hopeless situation: the world-power army behind, the impassable water ahead. Theme: the impossible situation that requires divine intervention. The disciples in the boat during the storm (Matthew 8:23-27) face the same structure: natural forces threatening destruction, the disciples powerless, the Deliverer present but apparently asleep. Jesus' act of stilling the storm is a Red Sea echo — the One who commands the sea does so as YHWH commanded the waters in the Exodus.
10: The Israelites were very afraid and cried out to YHWH — the fear before the miracle. The cry for help in the face of impossible odds is the prayer that precedes divine action. Theme: the disciples in the storm cry 'Lord, save us!' (Matthew 8:25); Peter sinking on the water cries 'Lord, save me!' (Matthew 14:30); the blind cried out to the Son of David (Matthew 20:31). The cry of the desperate is the prayer the Deliverer consistently answers throughout both Testaments.
11: The people's complaint to Moses — 'Were there not enough graves in Egypt? Why did you bring us out to die?' The murmuring in the face of apparent death. Theme: the human tendency to prefer familiar bondage over uncertain freedom. Jesus anticipates the same resistance: 'Jerusalem, Jerusalem, you who kill the prophets and stone those sent to you, how often I have longed to gather your children together, as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, and you were not willing' (Matthew 23:37). The people prefer Egypt to the wilderness, Barabbas to Jesus, the familiar to the kingdom.
12: 'It would have been better for us to serve the Egyptians than to die in the desert!' — the logic of slavery over the risk of freedom. A revelation of God by contrast: YHWH's response is not explanation but action. Moses does not win the argument; YHWH splits the sea. The resurrection does not answer death by argument but by event — it does not explain why suffering comes but overcomes it. The 'it would have been better' logic is always available to the generation that has not yet seen the miracle; the miracle makes the logic irrelevant.
13: 'Stand firm and see the salvation of YHWH' — the Hebrew word for salvation is yeshuah, the same root as Yeshua/Jesus. 'YHWH will fight for you; you need only be still.' A revelation of God that culminates in Christ: the salvation announced at the Red Sea is the salvation enacted on the cross. The people are told to be still and watch — the Deliverer will do what no human effort can accomplish. Matthew 1:21: 'he will save his people from their sins.' The name given to the Messiah is the name of the salvation proclaimed at the Red Sea.
14: YHWH will fight for you — the divine warrior fighting on behalf of the covenant people. Theme: Jesus as the divine warrior who fights the battles his people cannot fight. Ephesians 6:10-12: the spiritual warfare is against principalities and powers — not flesh and blood. Jesus' combat with Satan in the wilderness (Matthew 4:1-11), his authoritative casting out of demons, and supremely his harrowing of death in the resurrection — all are YHWH fighting for his people. The stillness required of Israel at the Red Sea is the same rest offered in the finished work of Christ (Hebrews 4:9-10).
15: YHWH tells Moses to stop crying out and move forward — the time for prayer has given way to the time for action. Revelation of God: prayer and obedient action are both appropriate in their time. The disciples are not told to pray for the paralyzed man — they are told to pick up the mat and walk (Mark 2:9-11). At Gethsemane, Jesus prays and then rises: 'Rise, let us go!' (Mark 14:42). The movement from prayer to action is the pattern of all who trust the divine word and step forward into the impossible.
16: Lift up your staff and stretch out your hand over the sea — the staff that divided water (cf. the Nile plague, the striking of the rock). The staff is the instrument of the divine authorization: Moses stretched out his hand in YHWH's name and the sea divided. A shadow: the cross is the instrument of divine authorization through which death is divided, the old creation separated from the new. As the staff divides the waters to make a way through, the cross makes a new and living way (Hebrews 10:20) through what was previously impassable — the 'way' through death into life.
17: YHWH will harden the hearts of the Egyptians so they pursue Israel into the sea — the destroyers drawn in by their own pursuit. Theme: 'he who digs a pit will fall into it' (Ecclesiastes 10:8); the powers of death drawn into the very death they try to impose. Revelation 20:14: 'death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire.' At the cross, death enters the place of death — and is swallowed by the resurrection. The Egyptian army pursuing into the divided sea is the type of death entering Christ's tomb: both are destroyed by the very site of their apparent triumph.
18: 'The Egyptians will know that I am YHWH when I gain glory over Pharaoh' — the witness dimension of the Exodus. The miracle of the Red Sea is designed not only to deliver Israel but to reveal YHWH to the nations. Theme: the cross and resurrection are the ultimate demonstration of who God is — not merely for the church's benefit but as the cosmic proclamation of divine character. Philippians 2:10-11: 'every tongue will confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father.' The glory gained at the Red Sea anticipates the glory declared at the resurrection.
19: The angel of God and the pillar of cloud moved from the front to the rear of the camp — positioned between Israel and Egypt. The divine presence as protective barrier. A shadow: Christ stands between the covenant people and their pursuer. Romans 8:38-39: 'neither death nor life... nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God that is in Christ Jesus our Lord.' The cloud that stood between Israel and Egypt is the type of the love in Christ that no hostile power can penetrate.
20: The cloud brought darkness to the Egyptians and light to Israel — simultaneously. The same divine presence that illuminates the covenant community obscures the way of those who oppose it. Theme: the same gospel that is the fragrance of life to those being saved is the fragrance of death to those who are perishing (2 Corinthians 2:15-16). The same cross that is the power of God to those being saved is foolishness to those who are perishing (1 Corinthians 1:18). The divine light and the divine darkness are the same reality from different angles.
21: Moses stretched out his hand and YHWH drove back the sea with a strong east wind — the miracle as YHWH working through natural means. A revelation of God: divine power often operates through the natural order rather than against it. The resurrection is the supreme act of divine power working within creation — not the soul escaping the body but the body transformed beyond death. The east wind that divides the sea is the natural-supernatural interface; the resurrection is the ultimate natural-supernatural event — physical, real, not resuscitation but transformation.
23: The Egyptians pursued and went into the sea after them — the hostile powers follow the delivered into the place of crossing. Theme: the powers of death, sin, and condemnation pursue those who have been delivered through the cross — but they enter the same water and are overthrown. Romans 8:1: 'there is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.' The pursuers who follow the delivered into the water of baptism are the powers of the old life — and they drown there. The crossing does not merely escape; it destroys what pursues.
24: In the morning watch YHWH looked down from the pillar of fire and cloud and threw the Egyptian army into confusion — divine intervention at the most vulnerable hour. The morning watch (3-6am) is the final darkness before dawn. Theme: the resurrection occurs 'early on the first day of the week, while it was still dark' (John 20:1). The divine intervention at the morning watch at the Red Sea and the resurrection at dawn on the first day echo each other — divine action in the pre-dawn darkness that changes everything.
25: YHWH jammed the wheels of the chariots so they drove heavily — the instruments of the enemy's power become the instruments of their destruction. Theme: God uses the enemy's own strength against them. The cross looked like the powers' instrument of destruction — they wielded it. But the cross became the instrument of their defeat (Colossians 2:14-15: 'having canceled the charge of our legal indebtedness... he has taken it away, nailing it to the cross... he made a public spectacle of them'). The chariot that became stuck is the type of the power that became its own defeat.
26: Stretch out your hand over the sea so that the waters may flow back over the Egyptians — the same command that divided the waters now reverses the division. The sea that opened to make a way for the delivered now closes to destroy the pursuers. Theme: the same death that Christ entered is the death that swallows what pursues him — 'death has been swallowed up in victory' (1 Corinthians 15:54, citing Isaiah 25:8). The waters that parted for Israel and closed on Egypt are the type of the death that Christ passes through and that death itself enters, only to be destroyed.
27: The sea returned to its normal state — the cosmic order restored after the miracle. Pharaoh's army is destroyed in the sea. A type: the return of the sea to its normal state after the crossing corresponds to the return to normal created order after the resurrection — not a suspension of nature but its restoration. The resurrection is not a miraculous interruption of creation but creation's fulfillment: 'the creation itself will be liberated from its bondage to decay' (Romans 8:21). The sea returning to its bed anticipates the new creation where all things are made right.
28: The waters came back and covered the chariots and horsemen — not one of them survived. The total destruction of the pursuing army is the definitive end of the old captivity. Theme: 'death has been swallowed up in victory' (1 Corinthians 15:54). The last enemy destroyed is death (1 Corinthians 15:26). The Egyptian army annihilated in the sea is the type of the powers and authorities disarmed and made a spectacle through the cross (Colossians 2:15). The enemies of the covenant people are not merely hindered but permanently removed.
29: The Israelites walked through the sea on dry ground with walls of water on both sides. A direct type: 1 Corinthians 10:1-2 explicitly identifies this crossing as a baptism — 'our ancestors were all under the cloud and all passed through the sea. They were all baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea.' The typological connection is apostolic, not allegorical. As Israel passed through the water from slavery to freedom, from Egypt to the wilderness covenant-journey, so believers pass through the waters of baptism from death to life, from the old humanity to the new (Romans 6:3-4: 'all of us who were baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death... just as Christ was raised from the dead, we too may live a new life').
30: YHWH saved Israel that day from the hand of the Egyptians — the great salvation event. The root of saved is yasha — the root of Yeshua/Jesus. What YHWH does at the Red Sea is named by the same word as the name given to the Messiah in Matthew 1:21 ('he will save his people from their sins'). The salvation of Israel from Egypt is the paradigmatic salvation event that the NT identifies as a type of the salvation accomplished in Christ — the same divine action, the same divine name, in its ultimate and final form.
31: Israel saw the great power that YHWH displayed against the Egyptians; the people feared YHWH and believed in him and in Moses his servant. The faith-response to the witnessed salvation event. Theme: the resurrection produces faith (John 20:29: 'blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed'; John 20:8: the beloved disciple saw and believed). The fear of YHWH — the reverential trust born of witnessed divine power — is the response the Exodus aims to produce, just as the miracles of Jesus are signs written down 'that you may believe' (John 20:31). Faith is generated by the witnessed mighty act of God in history.
